Detection Boundaries

What ESA detects. And what it doesn't.

No condition monitoring technology detects everything. Vibration misses submerged assets. Thermography misses internal faults. ESA has boundaries too. This page explains them: the physics, the measurement constraints, and what Samotics does to work within them.

Talk to an Engineer See what we do detect →
General Boundaries

Twelve constraints that apply to every deployment

These boundaries are inherent to Electrical Signature Analysis. They apply regardless of asset type, industry, or deployment size. Each includes what Samotics does to work within or around the constraint.

Three-phase AC induction motors

ESA is established and validated for three-phase AC induction motors. The diagnostic models, fault libraries, and signal processing are built around polyphase rotating fields. Single-phase and DC motors are not supported.

What Samotics does: Covers >95% of industrial motors above 5 kW. Synchronous motors supported on request.

Minimum motor size: 5 kW

Below 5 kW, motor current is typically too low for standard industrial CTs to resolve fault signatures above the noise floor. The real constraint is signal-to-noise ratio at the measurement chain, not motor size itself.

What Samotics does: SAM4 is optimised for motors 5 kW and above. Smaller motors may be supported with specialised CT sizing.

Low-speed assets: reduced confidence below 300 RPM

At low rotational speed, fault-related frequency components compress toward the supply frequency. Separating them requires longer analysis windows and higher frequency resolution. The real constraint is not RPM, but the spacing between fault sidebands and the fundamental.

What Samotics does: Custom signal processing for low-speed assets. Detection at 80% for Archimedes screws, improving with each release.

Load above 30% of rated capacity

Fault sidebands are proportional to motor slip, which increases with load. Below approximately 30% of rated load, slip is too low for classical spectral methods to separate fault signatures from the supply frequency.

What Samotics does: SAM4 flags low-load periods and excludes them from diagnostic windows. Advanced methods extend detection to lower load ranges for specific fault types.

VFD switching noise

Variable frequency drives introduce harmonics that can mask bearing fault frequencies and rotor bar sidebands. At low output frequencies (<30 Hz), classical spectral methods lose resolution. Detection confidence depends on VFD control mode and measurement location.

What Samotics does: SAM4 compensates for known VFD topologies. For VFD-fed assets, performance is validated per installation during commissioning.

Drive train complexity

Mechanical faults must propagate as torque variations through the drivetrain to reach the motor current. Direct-coupled loads produce the strongest signatures. Gearboxes, long shafts, and compliant couplings attenuate or reshape fault energy. Multi-stage transmissions reduce confidence.

What Samotics does: SAM4 maps each drive train at installation and sets detection expectations by topology: direct-coupled, single-stage gearbox, or multi-stage.

Supply voltage unbalance

Voltage unbalance above 2% creates current asymmetry that mimics or masks fault signatures. A 1% voltage unbalance can produce 6 to 10% current unbalance. ESA diagnostics for eccentricity and stator health become unreliable under sustained unbalance.

What Samotics does: SAM4 measures voltage and current on all three phases. Power quality issues are classified as a separate detection class, not confused with mechanical faults.

One motor per measurement channel

ESA requires current measurement on an individual motor feeder. Shared feeders or parallel motors on one breaker create crosstalk. Fault signatures from one motor appear in signals from neighbouring motors, making attribution impossible.

What Samotics does: SAM4 installation requires per-motor feeder access. This is verified during the coverage assessment before deployment.

CT sizing and accuracy class

Current transformers must have sufficient accuracy class and dynamic range for harmonic analysis. Protection-grade CTs are not suitable. Operating current must fall within the CT's guaranteed accuracy range, typically above 5% of rated primary current.

What Samotics does: SAM4 uses measurement-grade CTs specified per installation. CT sizing is part of deployment engineering.

Steady-state operating window

High-resolution spectral analysis requires a minimum period of stable speed and load, typically 45 to 120 seconds. Assets that never reach steady state require adapted signal processing methods.

What Samotics does: SAM4 classifies operating points automatically and selects stable windows. Time-frequency methods extend coverage to variable-duty assets.

Baseline period: operating envelope coverage

SAM4 needs baseline data covering the machine's normal operating points before full detection capability. Duration depends on how quickly the asset cycles through its load and speed range. Continuous-duty assets baseline in hours. Variable-duty assets take longer.

What Samotics does: Most deployments reach full detection within 48 hours of sufficient runtime across operating points.

Motor-specific measurement access

Current transformers must be installed on an individual motor feeder. The most common location is the motor control cabinet (MCC). Alternatives include VFD-embedded measurement or switchgear-level access. Physical access is mandatory during installation.

What Samotics does: After initial installation, all monitoring is remote. No ongoing site visits required.
Asset-Specific Boundaries

Detection rates vary by asset type

The general boundaries above set the physics. But detection accuracy also depends on the asset being monitored. Some faults produce stronger electrical signatures than others.

Asset Type Overall Detection Strongest Fault Known Limitation
Pumps 84.6% Cavitation, impeller wear Motor bearing: 30.8%. No direct seal leak detection
LV Motors 67.4% Misalignment, unbalance Small motors (<5 kW) limited
MV Motors Strong Insulation, rotor bar Partial discharge indicative only, not diagnostic
Fans & Blowers Good Unbalance, bearing (driven) Motor bearing: 50%. Blade defects: 66.7%
Conveyors Good Belt misalignment, bearing Belt tension below threshold
Compressors 37.5% Load profile changes Active development area. Variable-load profiles challenging
Transmissions Good Gear mesh, coupling Coupling: 57.1%. Cannot isolate specific gear stage
Archimedes Screws 80% Bearing, unbalance Low RPM weakens signatures. Trough wear below threshold
Canned Motor Pumps Early stage Thermal, flow anomalies No verified detection data yet. Sealed can attenuates signals
ESPs Developing Flow, electrical faults Cable length and VSD harmonics affect accuracy

For full detection boundaries per asset type, see the individual monitoring pages.

Explicit Non-Detections

Faults ESA cannot detect

Transparency builds trust. These fault types are outside ESA's detection capability, or require complementary technologies for reliable diagnosis.

Mechanical seal leaks

ESA does not detect seal leaks directly. The root causes that accelerate seal failure (misalignment, unbalance, cavitation) are among SAM4's strongest detection categories.

Partial discharge (diagnostic)

ESA provides a screening indicator for partial discharge on MV motors. It cannot replace dedicated PD testing. Use ESA for early warning, then confirm with offline insulation testing.

Power quality upstream of VFDs

SAM4 detects faults between the drive and the motor. Power quality issues upstream of the VFD are not visible in the motor current signal.

Process conditions without electrical impact

Pipe blockages, fluid temperature changes, and chemical composition shifts do not always produce detectable electrical signatures. Where they affect pump load, ESA detects the load change but cannot diagnose the process cause.

Structural faults without torque impact

A mechanical fault that does not create a cyclic torque variation on the motor shaft is invisible to ESA. Examples: structural resonance in a fan housing, foundation looseness that does not load the bearings cyclically.

Incipient bearing defects (Stage 1-2)

In the earliest stages of bearing failure, defect energy is primarily ultrasonic and does not modulate motor current detectably. ESA identifies bearing issues from Stage 3 onward. For early-stage detection on accessible assets, vibration analysis is more sensitive.

Simultaneous faults at similar frequencies

Two faults producing signatures at the same frequency can interfere destructively. This can cause underestimation of fault severity. SAM4's multi-feature approach reduces this risk but does not eliminate it.

Pushing the Boundaries

Where SAM4 extends beyond classical ESA limits

Classical ESA literature defines conservative boundaries. Samotics actively pushes them. Three areas where SAM4 operates beyond standard limits.

Low-load detection

Classical FFT methods fail below 30% load. SAM4 uses wavelet-based decomposition to isolate rotor fault signatures at lower load ranges by separating fault energy from the fundamental frequency component.

VFD harmonic isolation

Standard spectral analysis struggles with VFD-fed motors. SAM4 applies adaptive harmonic isolation to subtract VFD carrier harmonics. This restores bearing and rotor bar detection in inverter environments.

Transient startup analysis

During motor startup, the rotor passes through high slip values. Fault sidebands sweep across the frequency spectrum, making rotor bar defects visible in a 5 to 10 second window. SAM4 captures and analyses this transient.

Complementary, Not Competitive

Where ESA leads. Where vibration leads.

ESA and vibration are complementary technologies. Each has environments where it excels and environments where it struggles.

ESA leads when:

  • Assets are submerged, enclosed, or in hazardous zones
  • Physical access to the machine is impossible or expensive
  • Fleet-wide monitoring from a single MCC is needed
  • Electrical faults (insulation, phase imbalance, rotor bar) matter most
  • Continuous 24/7 monitoring without site visits is required

Vibration leads when:

  • Early-stage bearing defect classification (Stage 1-2) is critical
  • The asset is directly accessible and sensor mounting is practical
  • Structural resonance or foundation looseness needs diagnosis
  • Single high-value asset justifies dedicated instrumentation
  • Historical vibration baselines already exist
Most Samotics customers deploy ESA alongside existing vibration programmes. ESA covers the assets vibration cannot reach. Together, they close the monitoring gap.

Understand what SAM4 can detect in your plant.

Every deployment starts with a coverage assessment. Our engineers map your assets, identify which ones ESA can monitor, and set clear expectations on detection capability.
